summary: This is a link-post roundup covering three unrelated Mac topics. Gruber highlights Matt Neuburg's positive review of Mailsmith 2.0, praising Bare Bones Software's thorough revision of the email client. He shares a clever hack from a German magazine that enables external display support on iBooks (normally limited to mirroring). Finally, he notes that John Calhoun has released Glider Pro as freeware after the rights reverted from bankrupt publisher Casady & Greene.
key_points: Mailsmith 2.0 represents a significant improvement over 1.5, with Bare Bones doing painstaking professional work on speed, reliability, and interface organization
Recent iBooks have video hardware capable of driving external displays but are artificially limited to mirroring — a simple AppleScript hack unlocks the full capability
Glider Pro became freeware after publisher Casady & Greene went bankrupt and rights reverted to creator John Calhoun
money_quotes: Where I suffered through Mailsmith 1.5's drawbacks to take advantage of its special strengths, 2.0 is in every respect a delight to use.
The result is that Mailsmith 2.0 feels peppy, clean, crisp, and totally reliable.
When connected to an external display, iBooks are limited to mirroring, whereas PowerBooks can drive an external display in addition to their built-in screens.
But recent iBooks are equipped with powerful video cards that are capable of handling multiple displays.
Since Casady & Greene Inc. went bankrupt, the rights to the game Glider have reverted to me, the author.
key_insight: Apple's Mac software ecosystem in 2003 was rich with craftsmanship from independent developers, clever community hacks to unlock hidden hardware capabilities, and games finding new life as freeware after publisher bankruptcies.
